scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The Epistle of Barnabas outlines specific cultic and social norms for the emerging Christian community as distinct from Jewish practice.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The Epistle of Barnabas is generally dated to the late 1st or early 2nd century; 100 serves as a representative year for its contemporary ethical instructions.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text describes internal ritual regulations regarding priests and fasting for the 'new people' (Christians).
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to the author's floruit around 100 CE, as he is defining the cultic identity of his contemporary religious movement.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The claim regarding the abolition of circumcision is a core polemical rule defining the boundaries of the early Christian community against Jewish law.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This reflects the contemporary theological stance of the author during the late 1st/early 2nd century separation from Judaism.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The author is interpreting Old Testament types to establish symbolic and ritual logic for his own community.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Though referencing Israelite scripture, the author treats these as 'commandments' to be understood by his contemporary audience, dated to circa 100 CE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The author refers to the historical 'former people' and the Sinaitic covenant, placing the event in the past context of the Jewish people.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The verbatim refers to the breaking of the tables at Sinai, traditionally dated to the 13th century BCE; -1312 is a common traditional anchor for the Exodus era.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule explicitly defines the Christian group ('we') as the legitimate heirs of a new covenant inherited through Jesus.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This describes the contemporary status of the author's own group in the late 1st or early 2nd century CE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text defines the efficacy of Christian baptism, a contemporary ritual innovation of the group.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The author is describing the current religious practice and benefits of membership in his contemporary community circa 100 CE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The exclusion of Jews from baptismal remission is a contemporary polemical boundary set by the early Christian author.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This reflects the contemporary theological environment of the author at approximately 100 CE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The domestic duty of religious education is a social rule within the early Christian household.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This instruction is part of the 'Way of Light' ethical code for the author's contemporary community around 100 CE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text regulates the behavior of Christian masters toward their slaves within the church community.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This is a contemporary ethical norm for Christian households in the late 1st or early 2nd century.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The spiritual equality of slaves is a defining social-religious rule of the early Christian sect.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The author describes the logic of his contemporary community's social structure circa 100 CE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Mandatory obedience for Christian slaves is framed as a contemporary duty within the early church's social order.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This instruction is contemporaneous with the author's floruit in the late 1st or early 2nd century.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text promotes an ideal of economic koinonia (sharing) among members of the contemporary community.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Part of the author's ethical 'Way of Light' for his contemporaries around 100 CE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The author critiques the 'Way of Darkness,' which reflects his view of the corrupted external Roman legal and social system.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This critique reflects the author's observation of contemporary worldly society during his lifetime around 100 CE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The failure to protect widows and orphans is used to describe the contemporary 'Way of Darkness' in the broader society.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This represents the author's polemical view of the surrounding pagan or non-Christian society in 100 CE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Clement's letter describes the specific social hierachies and behaviors of the contemporary Corinthian church.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: 1 Clement is traditionally dated to 96 CE, during the reign of Domitian, reflecting contemporary church order.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The moral discipline of the young is presented as an established rule of the Christian community at Corinth.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Reflects the contemporary state of the church described by Clement in 96 CE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The instruction regarding household management defines the expected role of women within the contemporary Christian social order.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Clement is documenting the contemporary norms and instructions of the church in 96 CE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Restrictions on movement and the duty of domesticity are contemporary norms for women in the 1st-century church.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to the writing of the epistle in 96 CE, describing contemporary community expectations.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Clement presents the rescue of the wronged as the 'rule of tradition' (quoting Isaiah) for his contemporary audience to follow.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: While quoting scripture, Clement frames this as a contemporary moral imperative for the church in 96 CE.
